findings.message: bulk_create_items commits inside the per-item loop, so a failure partway through the batch leaves the database with a partial subset of items inserted — there is no atomicity across the batch. This violates the all-or-nothing invariant callers reasonably expect of a 'bulk' insert and can produce inconsistent state (e.g., the first N names persisted while later ones are lost, with no rollback).
findings.suggestion:
Add all items first, then commit once outside the loop so the batch is atomic. For example:

    def bulk_create_items(session: Session, names: list[str]) -> list[Item]:
        items = [Item(name=name) for name in names]
        session.add_all(items)
        session.commit()
        for item in items:
            session.refresh(item)
        return items

Optionally wrap in `with session.begin():` to make the transactional boundary explicit and ensure rollback on error.

findings.message: The function is annotated to return `list[Item]` but has no `return` statement, so it implicitly returns `None`. Callers relying on the documented return value will get `None` and fail (AttributeError on iteration / type-checker violation), and any partially-committed rows from the loop above will silently be discarded from the caller's perspective — compounding the partial-write data-integrity risk.
findings.suggestion: Return the accumulated list: add `return created` (or `return items` after the suggested atomic refactor) as the final statement of the function.
